Investors have poured billions into artificial intelligence being integrated with healthcare. Researchers have published promising results. Startups have raced to develop algorithms that can detect disease earlier and more accurately. Yet one question remains: once an AI […] The post HeartSciences opens AI marketplace for heart care appeared first on Longevity.Technology .

articles.title: What happens when AI starts designing aging therapies?
articles.summary: Immorta Bio says the future of medicine may lie in restoring youthful biology and artificial intelligence could be the architect. Modern medicine’s framework means waiting for a disease to appear, identifying what’s gone wrong and treating it. Heart disease gets one treatment; cancer gets another. Arthritis, dementia, kidney failure, etc. – each has its own […] The post What happens when AI starts designing aging therapies? appeared first on Longevity.Technology .

A growing international effort to examine aging through the lens of physics will come together in Cologne this summer, as researchers gather for Entropy in Aging, a conference and workshop hosted at the International House of […] The post Entropy and aging take center stage in Cologne appeared first on Longevity.Technology .

The FDA agreed on the study population, a 12 month treatment duration, the 11-item UMSARS Part I as the primary endpoint, and a […] The post Alterity’s rare disease drug slows progression by 48% ahead of final FDA trials appeared first on Longevity.Technology .

articles.title: Coya Therapeutics advances trial of new ALS treatment into next phase
articles.summary: Houston-based Coya Therapeutics has started the blinded active-treatment extension phase of the ALSTARS trial (NCT07161999). The Phase 2, randomized, multi-center, double-blind, placebo-controlled study is evaluating COYA 302 in people with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. Patients who completed the initial 24-week double-blind phase have entered a 24-week blinded extension during which all participants receive COYA 302; placebo […] The post Coya Therapeutics advances trial of new ALS treatment into next phase appeared first on Longevity.Technology .

According to the company, AlzeCure will receive a $10 million upfront payment, development and commercial milestone payments, and tiered mid-single-digit royalties on sales; the total deal value, excluding royalty payments, […] The post AlzeCure signs potential $1b Alzheimer’s drug deal with Eli Lilly appeared first on Longevity.Technology .

articles.title: MetaVia’s weekly obesity drug drives over 9% weight loss in 54 days
articles.summary: MetaVia presented late-breaking Phase 1 and preclinical data at the American Diabetes Association 2026 Scientific Sessions in New Orleans. The Phase 1 higher-dose (48 mg) cohort of DA-1726, a once-weekly GLP1R/GCGR dual agonist, produced mean body weight reductions of 6.1% at Day 26 and 9.1% at Day 54, waist circumference decreases of 5.8 cm and […] The post MetaVia’s weekly obesity drug drives over 9% weight loss in 54 days appeared first on Longevity.Technology .

articles.title: Custom protein binders zero in on near-identical disease targets with unprecedented selectivity
articles.summary: In the human body, the boundary between health and severe illness can be microscopic. For decades, molecular scientists have grappled with a frustrating biological reality: The proteins driving devastating diseases often look nearly identical to the ones keeping us alive.

articles.title: Degradable sensors reveal hidden soil secrets after microbes nibble on them
articles.summary: New degradable sensors could reveal insights into the secret—yet essential—microscopic life in soil. The sensors, which are being developed by soil scientists at Lancaster University and researchers at the University of Colorado Boulder and the University of Manchester, track biological activity in soil by using a degradable substrate that is nibbled on by microbes.

articles.title: New combined spore trapping and DNA sequencing technology tracks fungicide resistance in grain crops
articles.summary: Researchers at the Center for Crop and Disease Management (CCDM) have developed a new system that combines spore trapping with advanced DNA sequencing to confirm fungicide resistance from air samples containing airborne spores.

articles.title: Biopolymer beads extend fungus bioinsecticide shelf life and release
articles.summary: Researchers have used a biopolymer to encapsulate and extend the storage life and release rate of a bioinsecticidal fungus. The study is published in ACS Omega. The goal is to extend the shelf life of Beauveria bassiana, a fungus widely used as a bioinsecticide on various agricultural crops. Encapsulation is also a more sustainable alternative because it requires fewer applications and has less potential to affect nontarget species.

articles.title: AI model 'hears' Bryde's whale calls in seismic data from South China Sea
articles.summary: Researchers have repurposed an AI model designed for visual identification tasks to detect Bryde's whale calls contained within seismic data collected in the South China Sea. The detection system precisely identified calls more than 96% of the time and even identified some call events that were missed when the data were examined by hand, according to the study published in Seismological Research Letters.

articles.title: More sustainable agriculture: Recycled fertilizers could be part of the solution
articles.summary: Researchers have uncovered new insights into how phosphorus from recycled materials moves through soil—offering guidance to support more sustainable fertilizer use. Phosphorus is an essential nutrient for plant growth, yet many soils lack enough of it to support strong crop yields. Farmers often rely on fertilizers made from mined phosphorus, a limited, nonrenewable resource.

articles.title: Giant kelp's microscopic light antenna could inspire innovative climate solutions
articles.summary: New research reveals the microscopic machinery that helps giant kelp turn sunlight into energy, providing inspiration for innovative climate solutions. The study, published in Nature Communications, mapped one of the tiny antenna-like structures kelp uses for photosynthesis. Their unique architecture may hold the key to why kelp are the most efficient ocean organisms at capturing light and removing carbon dioxide from the atmosphere.

articles.title: Deep sea an untapped 'evolutionary engine' as dataset yields 500 million unique genes
articles.summary: The deep sea is a unique "evolutionary engine," with one of the richest and most unexplored sources of genetic diversity on Earth, according to a major new study that assessed its potential to transform biotechnology and DNA sequencing technologies.

articles.title: Study finds robotic mowers improve Florida lawn health while reducing maintenance
articles.summary: For many Florida homeowners, lawn mowing can feel like a never-ending chore, especially in the heat of summer. New guidance from experts suggests autonomous or robotic lawn mowers can ease that burden while effectively managing Florida's hardy lawns, including St. Augustine grass.

articles.title: Radar data can help protect birds from wind turbines
articles.summary: Wind turbines generate climate-friendly electricity, but they can pose a danger to migratory birds. A study led by the Swiss Federal Institute for Forest, Snow and Landscape Research (WSL) published in Nature Sustainability shows that weather radar data could be used to reduce the risk of collisions with only a minimal impact on electricity production: by strategically shutting down turbines when particularly large numbers of birds are in flight.
